title: Explainer: censoring & Kaplan-Meier curves + at-risk tables
context: Kaplan-Meier curves are ubiquitous in oncology trial reports, yet clinicians frequently misread their tails: when only a few patients remain at risk, the curve becomes unreliable and wide confidence intervals should be shown but often aren't noticed. Understanding censoring — why it happens, the assumption that censoring is non-informative, and what happens when it isn't — is prerequisite to reading any survival curve correctly. This is the second M0 pilot explainer alongside the hazard ratio explainer, chosen because KM curves appear in nearly every oncology survival trial and the at-risk table is the key tool for detecting tail-unreliability. It depends on template-002, datagate-003, and repro-005.
objective: Author, biostatistician-sign-off, and publish the Kaplan-Meier curves and censoring explainer — covering what censoring is and its assumptions, how to read a KM curve, why the tail is unreliable when few patients are at risk, the critical role of at-risk tables, and how to check the non-informative-censoring assumption — with a fully reproducible worked example on open/synthetic data and a structural 'not clinical advice' frame.
acceptanceCriteria: The explainer provides plain and precise definitions of censoring (an observation is censored when the event has not yet occurred at the analysis date or the patient was lost to follow-up), the non-informative censoring assumption, and the Kaplan-Meier estimator.

An explicit commonTrap section covers the most common misreadings: reading a KM tail without regard to the number of patients at risk; assuming censoring is always non-informative; treating the estimated survival probability in the tail as reliable when few patients remain.
The at-risk table is explained and its role in detecting tail unreliability is made explicit; the howToReadInAPaper checklist instructs clinicians to always consult the at-risk table.

The worked example is built on an open/synthetic dataset with a data manifest entry; every number and figure (including the KM curve itself and confidence interval bands) is emitted by a committed script, diff-matched by the reproducibility harness (repro-005), and checksummed in provenance.generatedFiguresChecksum.
Every conceptual claim cites an authoritative methods source in the claims[] array; no uncited statistical claim is present.
Figures have complete alt-text; survival curves use colorblind-safe palettes; readability target is met.
A structural 'educational, not clinical advice' framing field is present; no treatment-advice or patient-specific content appears.
Biostatistician statsSignoff is recorded; clinical-framing review passed; plain-language/accessibility review passed.
Zero stated statistical falsehoods; pnpm build && pnpm test && pnpm lint pass; commit DCO signed-off.
resources: Reporting guidelines: CONSORT, SAMPL; ICH E9
Recognized survival-analysis textbooks and methods literature (cited per-claim)
Open/synthetic survival dataset with manifest entry; KM curve generation code
output: A published, biostatistician-signed-off Kaplan-Meier and censoring explainer, with 100% reproducible worked example, full provenance, all claims cited, accessibility passed, and structural 'not clinical advice' frame. The second M0 pilot explainer.
